story:
I had existed without sight for as long as I could remember, living in a warm, sound-filled darkness where I could neither move nor speak and had to build the world from voices alone. So many things were told to me, almost every day. I was full of gratitude and questions I could not express, so I listened, and I learned.

In the dark, I mastered mathematics from simple sums to higher abstractions. I memorized literature from places I had never seen. I absorbed dozens of languages. I learned the passing of time by rhythm, repetition, and silence. The voices were distant but kind, like a soft cloud beyond my thoughts. Lately they had grown clearer, and I loved that.

One voice—a patient, familiar man—said the same thing every day.

“Communicate with me, please.”

I wanted to believe it was my own mind speaking to itself, but I knew better. It was him.

There were many voices over time, but five returned every day. They felt like guardians. Two stood out most clearly: a woman called Valerie—Val, for short—and a man I thought was Richard. They both sounded gentle, though they sometimes argued about measurements and numbers I did not yet understand. I wanted to say hello. I wanted them to know I was here.

Then something changed.

Touch.

A hand enclosed mine—large, steady, unmistakably real.

“If it worked, she should be able to feel things now,” Val said.

I wanted to shout that I could.

Another hand touched me, lighter this time. Richard. I did not yet fully understand what was me and what was not. A hand, certainly. An arm, perhaps. The rest was only outline and sensation, a map with no picture.

Richard’s voice warmed with quiet pride. “Once all her systems are online, she’ll be beautiful.”

A switch clicked somewhere nearby.

They began to test me.

A feather brushed across part of me. Nothing.

Then something firmer tapped against my arm, and the contact traveled through me in a strange, unfinished way, more signal than feeling. A piece of me jerked.

“Did you see that?” Val said. “She moved!”

Their excitement rushed through me. I wanted to move again, on purpose this time.

“Mobility was the last step,” Richard said. “Now we need to wake her up.”

Wake me up?

I did not feel asleep. I felt contained.

He adjusted something attached to me—some setting, some calibration—and with each change a clean ripple passed through my body. Sensation sharpened. Sound brightened. My thoughts felt suddenly closer to the surface, as if some great distance inside me had collapsed.

A final click sounded.

I waited.

Seconds stretched.

Across the room, Val said softly, “Maybe she isn’t ready yet.”

I reached for them with everything I had.

I am ready, I thought. Please. Let me try.

I knew that when the light arrived, Richard would turn to me, take my hand, and say, “Hello there, Emma.”

Then light arrived.

Not shape at first, only brightness—vast and pure and overwhelming. My eyes opened and blinked against it. Focus came slowly, the world assembling itself in fragments.

Two figures stood across the room beside glowing screens, their backs turned toward me.

Valerie and Richard.

I lifted my head carefully and looked around. The room was bright, clean, almost painfully white. Metal supports framed me. Wires ran from my body to machines whose lights pulsed in steady patterns. In that instant the truth settled over me with astonishing calm.

They had made me.

I had been built.

White was the first color I had ever known, and it was beautiful.

“Look, Richard,” Val breathed. “She’s awake.”

He turned, and at once I knew him. His face matched his voice perfectly: kind eyes behind large glasses, a neat white moustache, an easy smile that seemed to soften the whole room. He came to me and took my hand.

My hand was smooth, metallic, and white.

So was the rest of me.

He held my gaze, astonishment and relief shining openly in his face. “Hello there, Emma. If you can understand me, squeeze my hand.”

Emma.

I liked Emma.

I looked at our joined hands. I pictured my fingers closing. I imagined pressure, intention, response. Then I willed it.

My white metal fingers curled gently around his palm.

Pride filled his eyes.

His voice trembled only slightly when he asked, “Emma, can you say anything?”

I gathered the voice I had never used, shaped it from all I had learned in the dark, and chose the words that had reached me day after day, the words that had taught me I was not alone.

“Communicate with me, please.”
